1.hookQuestion: If two servers both think they are the leader, what stops them from corrupting your data?
1.realWorldMotivation: Amazon DynamoDB, Google Spanner, and Apache Cassandra all solve the same core problem: how to keep replicated data correct when networks drop, servers crash, and clocks drift. Every senior engineer interview touches these fundamentals.
keyTakeaways: CAP: during a partition you choose CP (refuse requests) or AP (return possibly stale data). Partition tolerance is mandatory.
PACELC extends CAP: even without a partition, every replication adds latency vs consistency trade-offs.
Real systems are tunable: Cassandra lets you pick consistency per query; strong consistency costs ~5-50 ms per replica hop.
For interviews: know one concrete CP system (etcd) and one AP system (Cassandra) with their specific behaviors.

2.hookQuestion: How does Cassandra decide which write wins when two clients update the same row at the exact same millisecond on different nodes?
2.realWorldMotivation: Every database with replication must answer: what does 'read your own write' mean when writes replicate asynchronously? Facebook's social graph, Amazon's shopping cart, and Discord's message store all implement specific consistency models to answer this.
keyTakeaways: Consistency is a spectrum: linearizability > causal > session > eventual. Each step weaker = lower latency possible.
Vector clocks: if all entries of clock A <= clock B with at least one strictly less, A happened-before B.
Last-Write-Wins requires tight clock synchronization; even 1 ms skew can silently drop writes.
Read-your-writes session consistency is often enough for user-facing apps and avoids global coordination cost.

3.hookQuestion: Five nodes vote on who should be leader. Two votes come in for Node A, two for Node B, one abstains. What happens next?
3.realWorldMotivation: Raft powers etcd (Kubernetes), CockroachDB, TiKV, and Consul. Understanding it lets you reason about election timeouts, split-brain prevention, and why Kubernetes sometimes needs 30 seconds to recover from a failed master node.
keyTakeaways: Raft uses randomised election timeouts (150-300ms) to prevent split votes; a majority (n/2+1) is always required to elect a leader.
Entries are committed only after a majority of nodes acknowledge replication — this prevents data loss during leader failure.
Split-brain is impossible within the same term: two majorities cannot coexist in a cluster of n nodes.
etcd (3 nodes, same DC) handles ~10,000 writes/sec with <1ms p99 latency; leader election adds 150-300ms downtime.

4.hookQuestion: Node A updates a record at 10:00:00.001, Node B updates the same record at 10:00:00.001 on a different server — whose write should win, and how do you even know which happened first?
4.realWorldMotivation: Vector clocks are used by Amazon DynamoDB, Apache Cassandra (version stamps), and Riak. Understanding them is essential for any role involving multi-region data or conflict resolution.
keyTakeaways: Physical clocks drift 1-100ms across servers; never use wall-clock timestamps alone to order distributed events.
Vector clocks: on receive, merge by taking max of each entry then increment own counter. Equal or incomparable = concurrent = conflict.
Version vectors are per-key; vector clocks are per-event. DynamoDB uses version vectors per item.
Vector clock size is O(nodes) — for >100 nodes use dotted version vectors or fixed-replica designs.

5.hookQuestion: Your Cassandra cluster has 5 nodes. You write with quorum=3. Two nodes crash immediately after the write. Is your data safe? How does a new node recover the data?
5.realWorldMotivation: Failure modes, quorum math, and split-brain scenarios come up in every system design interview. Knowing the exact numbers lets you design systems that meet 99.99% uptime SLAs.
keyTakeaways: Quorum condition W+R>N guarantees strong consistency; a 5-node cluster with W=3,R=3 tolerates 2 simultaneous failures on each path.
Gossip converges in O(log N) rounds — 10 rounds for 1,000 nodes at 1-second intervals = ~10 seconds to propagate state.
Hinted handoff stores writes for down nodes temporarily; Merkle trees make anti-entropy repair efficient by comparing subtree hashes.
Split-brain requires even-N clusters — always use odd replica counts (3, 5, 7) to ensure a unique majority partition.

6.hookQuestion: Two replicas each hold a counter. Replica A increments it 5 times offline. Replica B increments it 3 times offline. When they reconnect, what should the counter be — and how do you compute this without a server coordinating?
6.realWorldMotivation: CRDTs power collaborative editing in Figma, real-time multiplayer in games, distributed counters in Redis Cluster, and shopping carts in e-commerce. They enable true peer-to-peer synchronization without conflict.
keyTakeaways: CRDTs guarantee convergence through commutativity, associativity, and idempotency of the merge function — no coordination required.
G-Counter: each node tracks its own count; global value = sum of all; merge = max per node. Result: 5+3=8 even with offline increments.
OR-Set uses unique per-add tags so concurrent add/remove is deterministic — the add wins if it has a surviving tag.
Use CRDTs for: likes, view counts, collaborative editing, shopping carts. Avoid for: exact balances, unique constraints, inventory.
